root.narration: You're the runner on second base, one out, your team is down by a run. The count is 1-2 on the batter. The pitcher delivers a curveball and it bounces in the DIRT in front of home plate! The catcher drops down to block it but the ball kicks off to the left side, about 8 feet from the catcher. Do you advance to third?

choices.text: GO! The ball bounced away from the catcher -- I can make it to third!
Stay at second. It's too risky -- the catcher might recover quickly.
Go halfway and see what happens.

outcome-go-great.narration: You read the ball in the dirt perfectly! The moment you saw it bounce away from the catcher, you broke for third. The catcher scrambles to find the ball, picks it up, and throws to third -- but you're already sliding in! SAFE! Now you're on third base with one out. You can score on a fly ball, a ground ball, a wild pitch, or even a passed ball. Your heads-up baserunning just put you in scoring position!

outcome.headline: Heads-Up Baserunning! Advanced on the Dirt Ball!
outcome.explanation: Reading balls in the dirt is an elite baserunning skill. Here's what to watch for: 1) Did the ball bounce to the LEFT or RIGHT of the catcher? If it kicked sideways, the catcher has to move to get it -- that takes time. GO! 2) Did the ball bounce STRAIGHT down in front of the catcher? They probably blocked it and it's right in front of them -- stay put. 3) Did the ball go to the BACKSTOP? Definitely go! The key is watching the ball, not guessing. You should be taking your secondary lead and watching the pitch all the way to the catcher. If it hits the dirt and bounces away, REACT.

keyTerms.definition: A pitch that bounces before reaching the catcher. This creates an opportunity for baserunners to advance if the ball bounces away from the catcher.
Watching where the ball goes after it bounces in the dirt. Sideways or backward bounces = advance. Straight-down blocks = stay.
A wild pitch is the pitcher's fault (too far from the zone). A passed ball is the catcher's fault (should have caught it). Either way, smart runners can advance.

outcome.whatToRemember: Watch every pitch all the way to the catcher. If it bounces in the dirt and kicks sideways or to the backstop, REACT and advance. Read the ball, don't guess.

outcome-stay-okay.narration: You stay at second base. The catcher picks up the ball a few seconds later and looks around -- you're still at second. Safe play, but you missed a free base. The ball was 8 feet from the catcher -- you had time. The next pitch, the batter grounds out to short and you're stuck at second. Two outs now. If you'd been at third, that ground ball would have scored you.

outcome.headline: Free Base Wasted! Stayed at Second!
outcome.explanation: Staying was the safe play, but sometimes safe is too safe. The ball was 8 feet from the catcher -- that's plenty of distance to advance. In a one-run game, getting from second to third is HUGE because it opens up so many ways to score. You need to practice reading dirt balls so you can react instantly. The best baserunners in the game are always ready to advance on balls in the dirt. They're watching every pitch with the mindset of 'Can I go?'
outcome.whatToRemember: When a ball bounces away from the catcher, that's a free base waiting for you. Be ready on every pitch. The difference between second and third base is the difference between scoring and not scoring.

outcome-halfway-bad.narration: You go halfway between second and third and stop to watch. Bad idea! The catcher picks up the ball, sees you stranded between bases, and throws to the shortstop covering second. Now you're in a rundown! You try to make it to third but the shortstop throws to the third baseman -- TAG! You're out. Going halfway is the worst of both worlds. Your coach shakes their head: 'Either go or don't go. Never get stuck in the middle!'

outcome.headline: Caught in No-Man's Land! Out in Rundown!
outcome.explanation: The halfway approach is a trap. When you go halfway, you're too far from second to get back safely AND too far from third to advance safely. You're stuck. A good catcher will see you stranded and throw behind you. The rule is simple: GO or STAY. If the ball bounces far enough away, commit and sprint to third. If it stays near the catcher, stay at second. There is no 'halfway.' Make a decision and commit to it fully. Indecision on the basepaths gets you thrown out.
outcome.whatToRemember: On balls in the dirt, it's GO or STAY. Never go halfway. Read the bounce, make your decision instantly, and commit 100% to either advancing or staying.
